description: Wound healing phases, collagen biology, tensile strength, closure strategies, impediments
learning_objectives: Identify cells in each healing phase

questions.vignette: A surgical incision closed 3 days ago shows erythema and mild swelling.
questions.prompt: What is the PREDOMINANT cell type at post-operative day 3?

correct.explanation: By day 3, macrophages become predominant. They are 'master regulators' — phagocytose debris, secrete growth factors (PDGF, TGF-β, VEGF) that recruit fibroblasts and initiate proliferative phase. Wounds cannot heal without macrophages.
correct.mechanism: Timeline: Neutrophils peak 24-48hrs → macrophages predominate days 2-4 → fibroblasts predominate days 4-21 → myofibroblasts during contraction.

distractors.A: Neutrophils peak at 24-48 hours but are being replaced by day 3.
distractors.C: Fibroblasts predominate during proliferative phase (days 4-21), not yet at day 3.
distractors.D: Myofibroblasts appear later during wound contraction.

correct_pathway.question: Explain why 'Macrophages' is the best answer.
correct_pathway.expected_response: By day 3, macrophages become predominant. They are 'master regulators' — phagocytose debris, secrete growth factors (PDGF, TGF-β, VEGF) that recruit fibroblasts and initiate proliferative phase. Wounds cannot heal without macrophages.

correct_pathway.question: What is the underlying mechanism?
correct_pathway.expected_response: Timeline: Neutrophils peak 24-48hrs → macrophages predominate days 2-4 → fibroblasts predominate days 4-21 → myofibroblasts during contraction.

A.question: You chose 'Neutrophils'. Walk me through your reasoning.
A.expected_response: Neutrophils peak at 24-48 hours but are being replaced by day 3.

A.question: Now consider 'Macrophages'. What evidence supports this instead?
A.expected_response: By day 3, macrophages become predominant. They are 'master regulators' — phagocytose debris, secrete growth factors (PDGF, TGF-β, VEGF) that recruit fibroblasts and initiate proliferative phase. Wounds cannot heal without macrophages.

C.question: You chose 'Fibroblasts'. Walk me through your reasoning.
C.expected_response: Fibroblasts predominate during proliferative phase (days 4-21), not yet at day 3.

C.question: Now consider 'Macrophages'. What evidence supports this instead?
C.expected_response: By day 3, macrophages become predominant. They are 'master regulators' — phagocytose debris, secrete growth factors (PDGF, TGF-β, VEGF) that recruit fibroblasts and initiate proliferative phase. Wounds cannot heal without macrophages.

D.question: You chose 'Myofibroblasts'. Walk me through your reasoning.
D.expected_response: Myofibroblasts appear later during wound contraction.

D.question: Now consider 'Macrophages'. What evidence supports this instead?
D.expected_response: By day 3, macrophages become predominant. They are 'master regulators' — phagocytose debris, secrete growth factors (PDGF, TGF-β, VEGF) that recruit fibroblasts and initiate proliferative phase. Wounds cannot heal without macrophages.

questions.vignette: A patient 6 weeks post-surgery asks when her wound will be at maximum strength.
questions.prompt: What percentage of original tensile strength does a healed wound achieve at maximum?

correct.explanation: Healed wounds reach ~80% of original tissue strength at maximum (12 weeks to 1 year). Never 100%. At 3 weeks: ~20%. Type III collagen is replaced by type I with increased cross-linking during remodeling.
correct.mechanism: Tensile strength timeline: 1 week ~3%, 3 weeks ~20%, 6 weeks ~60%, 3 months ~80%. Scar collagen never perfectly recapitulates original organized matrix.

distractors.A: Wounds never achieve 100%. Scar matrix is disorganized.
distractors.D: 50% underestimates maximum. Strength is ~50% at 8-10 weeks but continues to ~80%.

correct_pathway.question: Explain why '~80%' is the best answer.
correct_pathway.expected_response: Healed wounds reach ~80% of original tissue strength at maximum (12 weeks to 1 year). Never 100%. At 3 weeks: ~20%. Type III collagen is replaced by type I with increased cross-linking during remodeling.

correct_pathway.question: What is the underlying mechanism?
correct_pathway.expected_response: Tensile strength timeline: 1 week ~3%, 3 weeks ~20%, 6 weeks ~60%, 3 months ~80%. Scar collagen never perfectly recapitulates original organized matrix.

A.question: You chose '100%'. Walk me through your reasoning.
A.expected_response: Wounds never achieve 100%. Scar matrix is disorganized.

A.question: Now consider '~80%'. What evidence supports this instead?
A.expected_response: Healed wounds reach ~80% of original tissue strength at maximum (12 weeks to 1 year). Never 100%. At 3 weeks: ~20%. Type III collagen is replaced by type I with increased cross-linking during remodeling.

Now consider '~80%'. What evidence supports this instead?
B.expected_response: Healed wounds reach ~80% of original tissue strength at maximum (12 weeks to 1 year). Never 100%. At 3 weeks: ~20%. Type III collagen is replaced by type I with increased cross-linking during remodeling.

D.expected_response: 50% underestimates maximum. Strength is ~50% at 8-10 weeks but continues to ~80%.

D.expected_response: Healed wounds reach ~80% of original tissue strength at maximum (12 weeks to 1 year). Never 100%. At 3 weeks: ~20%. Type III collagen is replaced by type I with increased cross-linking during remodeling.

questions.vignette: A pathology resident compares a 4-week scar to a 12-month scar.
questions.prompt: What is the primary collagen change during wound remodeling?

options.A: Type III replaced by type I with increased cross-linking
options.B: Type I replaced by type III for flexibility
options.C: Type IV replaces type III for basement membrane

correct.explanation: During remodeling (day 21+, lasting 1+ year), type III collagen is replaced by type I with greater tensile strength and cross-links. Mature scar type I:III ratio approaches but never equals normal skin.
correct.mechanism: Type III is thin (reticulin fibers), produced rapidly during proliferation. Type I is thicker with more cross-links. MMPs break down type III while new type I is deposited. Excessive deposition → hypertrophic scar or keloid.

distractors.C: Type IV is basement membrane, not involved in dermal scar remodeling.
distractors.D: Elastin is NOT regenerated in scars — why scars lack normal elasticity.

correct_pathway.question: Explain why 'Type III replaced by type I with increased cross-linking' is the best answer.
correct_pathway.expected_response: During remodeling (day 21+, lasting 1+ year), type III collagen is replaced by type I with greater tensile strength and cross-links. Mature scar type I:III ratio approaches but never equals normal skin.

correct_pathway.question: What is the underlying mechanism?
correct_pathway.expected_response: Type III is thin (reticulin fibers), produced rapidly during proliferation. Type I is thicker with more cross-links. MMPs break down type III while new type I is deposited. Excessive deposition → hypertrophic scar or keloid.

B.question: You chose 'Type I replaced by type III for flexibility'. Walk me through your reasoning.
Now consider 'Type III replaced by type I with increased cross-linking'. What evidence supports this instead?
B.expected_response: During remodeling (day 21+, lasting 1+ year), type III collagen is replaced by type I with greater tensile strength and cross-links. Mature scar type I:III ratio approaches but never equals normal skin.

C.question: You chose 'Type IV replaces type III for basement membrane'. Walk me through your reasoning.
C.expected_response: Type IV is basement membrane, not involved in dermal scar remodeling.

C.question: Now consider 'Type III replaced by type I with increased cross-linking'. What evidence supports this instead?
C.expected_response: During remodeling (day 21+, lasting 1+ year), type III collagen is replaced by type I with greater tensile strength and cross-links. Mature scar type I:III ratio approaches but never equals normal skin.

D.question: You chose 'Elastin replaces collagen for compliance'. Walk me through your reasoning.
D.expected_response: Elastin is NOT regenerated in scars — why scars lack normal elasticity.

D.question: Now consider 'Type III replaced by type I with increased cross-linking'. What evidence supports this instead?
D.expected_response: During remodeling (day 21+, lasting 1+ year), type III collagen is replaced by type I with greater tensile strength and cross-links. Mature scar type I:III ratio approaches but never equals normal skin.

questions.vignette: A 4 cm contaminated dog bite wound on the forearm, 14 hours old.
questions.prompt: What is the MOST appropriate closure strategy?

options.D: Delayed primary closure after 3-5 days of open wound management

correct.explanation: Contaminated bite wounds beyond the golden period (~6-8 hours) have high infection risk with primary closure. DPC: debride, open management 3-5 days, then close if no infection. Balances closure benefits with infection monitoring.
correct.mechanism: Primary closure traps bacteria → abscess. DPC is a compromise: mostly primary intention healing with 3-5 day safety delay. Granulation tissue is inherently infection-resistant.

distractors.A: Primary closure of contaminated bite wound >8 hours risks abscess.
distractors.B: Antibiotics don't substitute for proper wound management in contaminated wounds.
distractors.C: Skin grafting is for large tissue deficits, not a 4cm laceration.

correct_pathway.question: Explain why 'Delayed primary closure after 3-5 days of open wound management' is the best answer.
correct_pathway.expected_response: Contaminated bite wounds beyond the golden period (~6-8 hours) have high infection risk with primary closure. DPC: debride, open management 3-5 days, then close if no infection. Balances closure benefits with infection monitoring.

correct_pathway.question: What is the underlying mechanism?
correct_pathway.expected_response: Primary closure traps bacteria → abscess. DPC is a compromise: mostly primary intention healing with 3-5 day safety delay. Granulation tissue is inherently infection-resistant.

A.question: You chose 'Primary closure immediately'. Walk me through your reasoning.
A.expected_response: Primary closure of contaminated bite wound >8 hours risks abscess.

A.question: Now consider 'Delayed primary closure after 3-5 days of open wound management'. What evidence supports this instead?
A.expected_response: Contaminated bite wounds beyond the golden period (~6-8 hours) have high infection risk with primary closure. DPC: debride, open management 3-5 days, then close if no infection. Balances closure benefits with infection monitoring.

B.question: You chose 'Primary closure with prophylactic antibiotics'. Walk me through your reasoning.
B.expected_response: Antibiotics don't substitute for proper wound management in contaminated wounds.

B.question: Now consider 'Delayed primary closure after 3-5 days of open wound management'. What evidence supports this instead?
B.expected_response: Contaminated bite wounds beyond the golden period (~6-8 hours) have high infection risk with primary closure. DPC: debride, open management 3-5 days, then close if no infection. Balances closure benefits with infection monitoring.

C.question: You chose 'Skin grafting'. Walk me through your reasoning.
C.expected_response: Skin grafting is for large tissue deficits, not a 4cm laceration.

C.question: Now consider 'Delayed primary closure after 3-5 days of open wound management'. What evidence supports this instead?
C.expected_response: Contaminated bite wounds beyond the golden period (~6-8 hours) have high infection risk with primary closure. DPC: debride, open management 3-5 days, then close if no infection. Balances closure benefits with infection monitoring.

questions.vignette: A 22-year-old woman develops a raised scar after ear piercing that extends beyond the piercing site.
questions.prompt: What distinguishes this from a hypertrophic scar?

options.A: Keloids are painful, hypertrophic are not
options.B: Keloids extend beyond wound boundaries, hypertrophic stay within them
options.C: Keloids occur only in dark-skinned individuals
options.D: Hypertrophic scars never regress, keloids can

correct.explanation: The key distinguishing feature: keloids extend BEYOND wound margins into surrounding normal skin. Hypertrophic scars remain WITHIN original wound boundaries. Keloids have disorganized thick collagen and rarely regress; hypertrophic scars often improve over 1-2 years.
correct.mechanism: Both from excessive collagen. Hypertrophic: organized bundles parallel to skin, respect boundaries. Keloids: disorganized thick bundles, extend beyond. Keloid excision has 50-80% recurrence.

distractors.A: Both can be painful or pruritic. Pain is not distinguishing.
distractors.C: More common in darker skin but can occur in ANY race.
distractors.D: Opposite: hypertrophic often regress over 1-2 years, keloids rarely do.

correct_pathway.question: Explain why 'Keloids extend beyond wound boundaries, hypertrophic stay within them' is the best answer.
correct_pathway.expected_response: The key distinguishing feature: keloids extend BEYOND wound margins into surrounding normal skin. Hypertrophic scars remain WITHIN original wound boundaries. Keloids have disorganized thick collagen and rarely regress; hypertrophic scars often improve over 1-2 years.

correct_pathway.question: What is the underlying mechanism?
correct_pathway.expected_response: Both from excessive collagen. Hypertrophic: organized bundles parallel to skin, respect boundaries. Keloids: disorganized thick bundles, extend beyond. Keloid excision has 50-80% recurrence.

A.question: You chose 'Keloids are painful, hypertrophic are not'. Walk me through your reasoning.
A.expected_response: Both can be painful or pruritic. Pain is not distinguishing.

A.question: Now consider 'Keloids extend beyond wound boundaries, hypertrophic stay within them'. What evidence supports this instead?
A.expected_response: The key distinguishing feature: keloids extend BEYOND wound margins into surrounding normal skin. Hypertrophic scars remain WITHIN original wound boundaries. Keloids have disorganized thick collagen and rarely regress; hypertrophic scars often improve over 1-2 years.

C.question: You chose 'Keloids occur only in dark-skinned individuals'. Walk me through your reasoning.
C.expected_response: More common in darker skin but can occur in ANY race.

C.question: Now consider 'Keloids extend beyond wound boundaries, hypertrophic stay within them'. What evidence supports this instead?
C.expected_response: The key distinguishing feature: keloids extend BEYOND wound margins into surrounding normal skin. Hypertrophic scars remain WITHIN original wound boundaries. Keloids have disorganized thick collagen and rarely regress; hypertrophic scars often improve over 1-2 years.

D.question: You chose 'Hypertrophic scars never regress, keloids can'. Walk me through your reasoning.
D.expected_response: Opposite: hypertrophic often regress over 1-2 years, keloids rarely do.

D.question: Now consider 'Keloids extend beyond wound boundaries, hypertrophic stay within them'. What evidence supports this instead?
D.expected_response: The key distinguishing feature: keloids extend BEYOND wound margins into surrounding normal skin. Hypertrophic scars remain WITHIN original wound boundaries. Keloids have disorganized thick collagen and rarely regress; hypertrophic scars often improve over 1-2 years.

questions.vignette: A 65-year-old diabetic with chronic non-healing foot wound. HbA1c 10.2%, chronic prednisone, albumin 2.4 g/dL.
questions.prompt: Which factor is MOST critical to address?

correct.explanation: Poorly controlled diabetes affects every healing phase: impaired neutrophil function, decreased angiogenesis, impaired fibroblast proliferation, reduced collagen synthesis. Plus neuropathy and microvascular disease. HbA1c >8% consistently associated with impaired healing.
correct.mechanism: Hyperglycemia: AGEs damage vessels, impaired WBC function, peripheral neuropathy, microvascular disease, glycosylated collagen dysfunction.

distractors.B: Steroids impair healing but secondary to multi-system diabetes impact.
distractors.C: Albumin <2.5 indicates malnutrition impacting healing, but less impactful than uncontrolled diabetes.
distractors.D: Not equally impactful — glycemic control has the largest single effect.

correct_pathway.question: Explain why 'Glycemic control (HbA1c 10.2%)' is the best answer.
correct_pathway.expected_response: Poorly controlled diabetes affects every healing phase: impaired neutrophil function, decreased angiogenesis, impaired fibroblast proliferation, reduced collagen synthesis. Plus neuropathy and microvascular disease. HbA1c >8% consistently associated with impaired healing.

correct_pathway.question: What is the underlying mechanism?
correct_pathway.expected_response: Hyperglycemia: AGEs damage vessels, impaired WBC function, peripheral neuropathy, microvascular disease, glycosylated collagen dysfunction.

B.question: You chose 'Corticosteroid use'. Walk me through your reasoning.
B.expected_response: Steroids impair healing but secondary to multi-system diabetes impact.

B.question: Now consider 'Glycemic control (HbA1c 10.2%)'. What evidence supports this instead?
B.expected_response: Poorly controlled diabetes affects every healing phase: impaired neutrophil function, decreased angiogenesis, impaired fibroblast proliferation, reduced collagen synthesis. Plus neuropathy and microvascular disease. HbA1c >8% consistently associated with impaired healing.

C.question: You chose 'Malnutrition (albumin 2.4)'. Walk me through your reasoning.
C.expected_response: Albumin <2.5 indicates malnutrition impacting healing, but less impactful than uncontrolled diabetes.

C.question: Now consider 'Glycemic control (HbA1c 10.2%)'. What evidence supports this instead?
C.expected_response: Poorly controlled diabetes affects every healing phase: impaired neutrophil function, decreased angiogenesis, impaired fibroblast proliferation, reduced collagen synthesis. Plus neuropathy and microvascular disease. HbA1c >8% consistently associated with impaired healing.

D.question: You chose 'All equally important'. Walk me through your reasoning.
D.expected_response: Not equally impactful — glycemic control has the largest single effect.

D.question: Now consider 'Glycemic control (HbA1c 10.2%)'. What evidence supports this instead?
D.expected_response: Poorly controlled diabetes affects every healing phase: impaired neutrophil function, decreased angiogenesis, impaired fibroblast proliferation, reduced collagen synthesis. Plus neuropathy and microvascular disease. HbA1c >8% consistently associated with impaired healing.
